title: Demonstratives & Possessives

primarySong.title: Mi Gente
primarySong.note: Final song locked by team-lead at Gate 1 (2026-05-19). Title IS the lesson — 'Mi Gente' bundles the first-person possessive (mi) with a masculine noun (gente) in two words, repeated dozens of times across the track. Pop = 'Mi Persona Favorita' (Alejandro Sanz & Camila Cabello); reggaeton = 'Mi Gente' (J Balvin & Willy William); R&B = 'Tu Falta De Querer' (Mon Laferte); regional Mexican = 'Esta Noche Con Mariachis' (Vicente Fernández). Every genre variant title carries a Module 8 demonstrative or possessive on its face.

notes: Module 8 audio set — narration-first per Reine's vision (locked 2026-05-15). Scripts lifted verbatim from module-008-content.ts.draft ttsTriggers array. M5/M6/M7 gold-standard style — full English sentences with Spanish tokens embedded, single MP3 per cue, no slow/normal pairs. 9 narration cues + 6 reading-token cues = 15 files total. Reading-passage cap (6 unique tappables) enforced by team-lead at Gate 1 and verified character-by-character against draft lines 144-179. Speed rule applied strictly: all narration ≥ 4 words → speed 1.0 (no exceptions, M3/M6 stretching-artifact prevention). Reading-tokens: 'Mi Gente' (2 words → 0.95, title-cased to match passage tappable exactly); single-word tokens (mi / tu / su / esta / aquel → 0.9). Reading-token text matches readingPassage tappable text EXACTLY (case + accents) — note the passage tappable is title-cased 'Mi Gente' (lines 149), not 'mi gente'; the draft's cue list used lowercase but the rule is mirror-the-tappable, so this manifest uses 'Mi Gente'. M8 is the LAST FREE MODULE before paywall — lesson-complete narration is the graduation moment and must land hard.

ttsTriggers.text: Today we're learning the two systems Spanish uses to talk about the things around you. The first is demonstratives — este, ese, aquel — three words for three distances. The second is possessives — mi, tu, su — for who the thing belongs to. As you listen to 'Mi Gente' by J Balvin and Willy William, count every time you hear mi. The title itself is the lesson — mi gente means my people, and the whole song is one syllable repeated like a flag. Press listen when you're ready.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, teacherly — sets up the lesson, primes the song
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Mixed-language opener. nova reads English as American English; Spanish tokens (este, ese, aquel, mi, tu, su, Mi Gente, mi gente, J Balvin, Willy William) with Spanish phonemes. The three demonstratives must sound clearly distinct: 'este' (ES-teh), 'ese' (EH-seh), 'aquel' (ah-KEL — stress on the second syllable, no accent mark needed since the stress is on the final closed syllable per default Spanish rules). The three possessives are short, open vowels: 'mi' (mee), 'tu' (too), 'su' (soo). 'Mi Gente' (mee HEN-teh — soft Spanish g before e, NOT English g) appears as the song title and must sound clean and Spanish. 'J Balvin' (hota bal-BEEN — stage name, the J pronounced as the Spanish letter name 'hota' or simply 'jay' as he's marketed bilingually; 'jay BAL-vin' is acceptable). 'Willy William' (English pronunciation — French-Moroccan producer, stage name uses English phonetics). Estimated duration: ~27s.

ttsTriggers.text: Demonstratives say how far away something is. Este — this, right here in my hand. Ese — that, over by you. Aquel — that one over there, far from both of us. The feminine forms are esta, esa, and aquella — they match feminine nouns the way Module 7 taught. Three distances, two genders, six little words that let you point at anything.
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Six demonstrative forms must each sound distinct. Masculine row: 'este' (ES-teh), 'ese' (EH-seh — slightly more open first vowel than este), 'aquel' (ah-KEL — stress on final syllable). Feminine row: 'esta' (ES-tah), 'esa' (EH-sah), 'aquella' (ah-KEH-yah — the ll is a soft y in Latin American Spanish, NOT a Castilian ʎ). The contrast between este/esta and ese/esa is one vowel each — must be enunciated cleanly so the learner can hear the gender swap. 'aquella' has stress on the middle syllable. Estimated duration: ~13s.

ttsTriggers.text: Possessives say who the thing belongs to. Mi means my — mi libro, my book. Tu means your, informally — tu canción, your song. Su means his, her, your formal, or their — all four at once. Su libro could be his book, her book, your book, or their book, and Spanish lets context decide. The plural forms are mis, tus, and sus — they match the noun, not the owner.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: clear, instructive — teach-the-possessive-system tone with su-ambiguity flagged
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Three singular possessives: 'mi' (mee — short closed i), 'tu' (too — short closed u, NO accent so it's the possessive not the pronoun tú), 'su' (soo — short closed u). Three plural forms: 'mis' (mees), 'tus' (toos), 'sus' (soos) — each adds one clean s. Example pairs: 'mi libro' (mee LEE-bro — single-tap r), 'tu canción' (too can-SYOHN — stress on final syllable per accent), 'su libro' (soo LEE-bro). The su-ambiguity sentence ('his, her, your formal, or their — all four at once') is the conceptual core and must be paced clearly with comma beats between each English gloss. Estimated duration: ~14s.

ttsTriggers.text: Here's the trick songwriters love. Este and esta open a line with right now — esta noche means this night, the one happening as I'm singing to you. Aquel and aquella reach back across time — aquel verano means that summer, the one from years ago, the one I remember. English needs extra words to do that — 'that summer back then, the one I remember' — Spanish does it in one syllable. Every time you hear aquel in a song, the singer is reaching across distance, either in space or in memory.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, reflective — the conceptual crux, songwriting-as-grammar framing
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Two anchor phrases must sound clean and contrast in distance: 'esta noche' (ES-tah NO-cheh — open vowels, soft ch) — feels close. 'aquel verano' (ah-KEL beh-RAH-no — note the v in verano is pronounced like a soft Spanish b, the rolled r in verano is single-tap, stress on the middle syllable) — feels distant. The repetition of 'aquel' in the closing line is the takeaway — must sound consistent with the first occurrence. Estimated duration: ~17s.

ttsTriggers.text: 'Es mi canción favorita.' — It's my favorite song. Four words wiring together three modules. Es is the ser form for 'it is', from Module 6. Mi is the first-person possessive, from today. Canción is feminine, so favorita has to end in -a to agree with it — Module 7's gender system at work. From today on, this is the sentence you can say about every track in Melodia. It's my favorite song. The grammar is yours.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, conversational — modeling a sentence that ties three modules together
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Opening sentence is the learner's takeaway — must sound like a natural declarative, not a flat recitation. 'Es mi canción favorita' (es mee can-SYOHN fa-bo-REE-tah — note v in favorita is a soft Spanish b, single-tap r, stress on the second syllable of favorita, accent on the ó in canción dictates final-syllable stress). The breakdown beats — 'Es is the ser form', 'Mi is the first-person possessive', 'Canción is feminine' — should each enunciate the Spanish token cleanly so the learner hears the component being labeled. Estimated duration: ~15s.

ttsTriggers.text: Try this. Look around your room one more time — you did this yesterday with articles. Now say each object twice. First with a demonstrative, pointing — este libro, esa lámpara, aquel cuadro. Then with a possessive, claiming it — mi libro, mi lámpara, mi cuadro. Now pick one object and imagine handing it to someone. Say 'Es para ti — es tu libro ahora.' It's for you — it's your book now. You're not pointing at objects anymore. You're giving them away.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, encouraging — coaching the learner from pointing to claiming to giving
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Three demonstrative+noun pairs: 'este libro' (ES-teh LEE-bro), 'esa lámpara' (EH-sah LAHM-pa-ra — stress on first syllable per accent), 'aquel cuadro' (ah-KEL KWAH-dro — the cu glide is critical, single-tap r). Three possessive+noun pairs: 'mi libro' (mee LEE-bro), 'mi lámpara' (mee LAHM-pa-ra), 'mi cuadro' (mee KWAH-dro). Closing sentence 'Es para ti — es tu libro ahora' (es PAH-rah tee — es too LEE-bro ah-OH-rah) is the emotional pivot from pointing to giving and must be paced with a beat between the two clauses. 'ti' (tee — object pronoun, NO accent), 'ahora' (ah-OH-rah — single-tap r). Estimated duration: ~17s.

ttsTriggers.text: J Balvin grew up in Medellín, Colombia, and built his career one dance floor at a time before becoming one of the most recognized voices in reggaeton anywhere in the world. In 2017, he released a song with the French-Moroccan producer Willy William that crossed borders most Spanish-language tracks never quite manage to cross — Mi Gente. The title is today's whole lesson in two words. Mi is the first-person possessive — my — and gente means people, so the phrase together claims a whole community as the speaker's own. That single syllable is everywhere in Spanish-language music. When an artist sings about tu corazón, they're naming your heart and addressing you directly at the same time. When they sing about su familia, they could mean his family, her family, your family, or their family — the listener has to read the rest of the verse to know which. Demonstratives do something different. Esta noche means tonight — the night happening right now, close enough to touch. But aquel verano means that summer — the one from years ago, far enough away to be a memory. Spanish gives you three distances where English gives you two, and songwriters use that extra word to reach across time. By the end of 'Mi Gente', you'll have heard mi dozens of times. That's J Balvin claiming Medellín, claiming his crew, claiming everyone listening — one syllable, one whole city.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, reflective — narration tone for the artist/cultural bio
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Long-form narration — 4+ word text MUST be 1.0 (donde-jugaran stretching prevention from M3/M6). Spanish proper nouns must be pronounced cleanly: 'J Balvin' (hota bal-BEEN or jay BAL-vin — bilingual stage name; consistent with intro cue), 'Medellín' (meh-deh-YEEN — note ll is soft y in Latin American Spanish, stress on the final syllable per the accent mark, two occurrences in passage must sound identical), 'Colombia' (English exonym — co-LUM-bee-uh), 'Willy William' (English pronunciation). 'Mi Gente' (mee HEN-teh — soft Spanish g before e, two occurrences must sound identical). The six Spanish tappable tokens (Mi Gente, mi, tu, su, esta, aquel) scattered through the passage must each be clean Spanish — these are the same tokens the learner will tap on screen. Embedded phrases the learner won't tap but must still hear cleanly: 'tu corazón' (too co-rah-SOHN — final-syllable stress), 'su familia' (soo fa-MEE-lyah), 'esta noche' (ES-tah NO-cheh), 'aquel verano' (ah-KEL beh-RAH-no). Estimated duration: ~85s at 1.0x.

ttsTriggers.text: If you picked pop, your song today is 'Mi Persona Favorita' — a duet by Alejandro Sanz and Camila Cabello. The title is a textbook M8 phrase — mi wrapped around persona favorita, the whole thing claiming someone as yours. The arrangement is slow, the flamenco guitar leaves space between every line, and the word mi lands clean every time it arrives. Each one is the speaker claiming the listener as their favorite — the entire emotional architecture of the song is built on one syllable.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, reflective — narration tone, pair-toned with the reading passage
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: CONDITIONAL cue — only plays when user.genre === 'pop'. Spanish-token QA: 'Mi Persona Favorita' (mee per-SOH-nah fa-bo-REE-tah — four clean Spanish words, stress on the second syllable of persona, second syllable of favorita, v in favorita is a soft Spanish b). 'Alejandro Sanz' (ah-leh-HAN-dro sanz — single-tap r, soft Spanish j sounds like English h, stage name's Sanz is enunciated as Spanish 'sahnz' not Anglicized). 'Camila Cabello' (cah-MEE-lah cah-BEH-yo — note ll in Cabello is soft y in Latin American Spanish, stress on the middle syllable of Cabello, Cuban-American artist whose name is commonly pronounced this way bilingually). The repeated 'mi' tokens in the script must sound consistent with the mi used in the intro and vocab cues. Stretch goal — generate if budget permits, otherwise defer. Estimated duration: ~14s.

ttsTriggers.text: Look at what you can do now. You can name anything in Spanish — that was Module 7. You can point at it from three different distances — este, ese, aquel — and you can claim it for any of five people — mi, tu, su, nuestro, su. Every noun phrase in A1 Spanish is built from the tools you have right now. You started this lesson saying words. You're finishing it building sentences out of grammar that belongs to you. This is the last free module — and you earned every part of it. From here on, the language opens up. Mi gente. That's you now.
ttsTriggers.voiceStyle: warm, celebratory, slightly more emphatic than M7 — graduation moment closing the free tier; 'Mi gente. That's you now.' must land as the emotional payoff
ttsTriggers.pronunciationNotes: Closing cue — LAST FREE MODULE graduation moment. Should feel like a coach calling the student into the next phase, not a reading. Three demonstratives in a roll (este / ese / aquel) and five possessives in a roll (mi / tu / su / nuestro / su) — each list paced with brief comma beats, NOT rushed. 'nuestro' (NWES-tro — the nw glide is critical, single-tap r, stress on the first syllable). The repeated 'su' is intentional (covers both 'his/her/your formal' and 'their' — the ambiguity is the lesson) and must sound identical both times. Final two lines 'Mi gente. That's you now.' is the payoff — the 'Mi gente' (mee HEN-teh) must be enunciated with weight, a beat of silence after, then 'That's you now.' in a slightly softer register. This sentence carries the paywall transition emotional weight — do NOT let TTS flatten the cadence. Estimated duration: ~22s.
